papers.title: Everyday Dyadic Coping in Aging Couples: The Effects of Positive and Negative Coping Behaviors and Their Discrepancy on the Stress-Reactivity Process.
papers.journal: The journals of gerontology. Series B, Psychological sciences and social sciences
papers.abstract: OBJECTIVES: Although spousal support is critical for managing older adults' long-term care dependence, the role of different spousal coping behaviors in stress reactivity remains unclear. This study aimed to examine the effects of positive and negative spousal coping behaviors and partner discrepancies on stress reactivity among older adults in China. METHODS: Using 8,385 observations, an ecological momentary assessment study (N = 107 couples) investigated the occurrence of daily stressors and the immediate effect on negative affect (NA), as well as the moderating roles of spousal coping behaviors and their discrepancies in older adults. We measured four types of daily stressors, NA, spousal supportive, and negative coping behaviors as reported by each partner, and discrepancies in these coping behaviors between partners. RESULTS: Daily stressors were associated with higher NA, with health and home-related stressors showing the most consistent effects across partners. Supportive coping behaviors buffered the impact of three stressor types on NA for both partners. However, when wives experienced health or home-related stressors and perceived higher negative coping behaviors from their husbands, their NA reactivity increased. By contrast, greater discrepancies in coping behaviors, whether supportive or negative, significantly moderated NA only among husbands. DISCUSSION: These findings highlight the importance of fostering supportive coping strategies in daily interactions among older couples and suggest that interventions should be tailored to gendered coping mechanisms. Specifically, enhancing husbands' supportive coping may ease wives' emotional burden, while promoting similarity in coping may protect husbands from heightened negative affect.

papers.title: Time Management, Stigma, Jealousy, and More: A Content Analysis of the Challenges of Polyamory.
papers.abstract: Despite growing public and scientific interest in consensually non-monogamous (CNM) relationships, clinical and empirical understandings of the challenges faced by people who engage in polyamory remain limited. To address this gap, we conducted a content analysis of 763 open-ended responses from 219 individuals currently in a polyamorous relationship who were asked to describe the challenges of their relationship structure. We identified 15 majors theme and several corresponding minor themes. The four most frequently reported challenges of polyamory included: (1) time management issues (72.6%), including logistical complexity and feeling "spread too thin;" (2) stigma and negative evaluations (48.0%), particularly from society, friends, and family; (3) jealousy (35.2%), involving one's own feelings, those of partners, and metamours (a partner's other partners); and (4) the emotionally demanding nature of polyamory (27.4%), such as increased relational complexity and emotional labor. The challenges of polyamory identified reflect both structural and interpersonal stressors that may be presenting concerns or arise in therapy. These findings support the need for culturally competent and CNM-affirming mental health practices.

papers.title: The Worst Tie Matters: Intergenerational Relationship Trajectories in Multi-Child Families and Depressive Symptoms of Older Parents in China.
papers.journal: The journals of gerontology. Series B, Psychological sciences and social sciences
papers.abstract: OBJECTIVES: Close intergenerational relationships are associated with better mental health in later life, yet limited longitudinal research has examined how trajectories of relationship quality, especially differentiation across multiple children within the same family, relate to older parents' depressive symptoms. This study identifies distinct trajectories of parent-child relationship quality and examines their associations with depressive symptoms among older adults in China, while assessing whether these associations vary across socioeconomic groups. METHODS: Using nationally representative panel data from the China Longitudinal Aging Social Survey (2014-2020; N = 3,519), we applied group-based trajectory modeling to characterize six-year patterns of relationship quality, separately capturing the worst, best, and average ties within families. Linear regression models estimated associations between these trajectories and depressive symptoms at the final wave, adjusting for baseline characteristics and testing whether these associations differed by education, occupation, and hukou status. RESULTS: Parents in steadily or rapidly strengthening relationship trajectories reported significantly fewer depressive symptoms than those in persistently strained trajectories. When trajectories of the worst, best, and average ties were modeled simultaneously, only trajectories of the worst relationship remained significantly associated with depressive symptoms. These associations were concentrated among parents with lower socioeconomic status. DISCUSSION: Intergenerational relationship dynamics, particularly changes in the most strained tie, are linked to older adults' mental health in multi-child families. Findings highlight the psychological importance of relational repair and suggest that parents with fewer socioeconomic resources may be especially sensitive to relationship change.
